repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
Part of #4029

There's a few Cython-specific decisions to flag: 
* typed memoryviews are treated as sequences
* C tuples are treated as sequences
* Some constant length sequences are resolved at compile-time and other branches dropped (mainly for ctuples)

@@ -80,27 +84,54 @@ def refactor_cases(self):
             new_cases.append(SubstitutedMatchCaseNode(
                 current_if_statement.pos, body=current_if_statement
             ))
-        self.cases = new_cases
+        # eliminate optimized cases
+        self.cases = [c for c in new_cases if c is not None]
 
     def analyse_declarations(self, env):
         self.subject.analyse_declarations(env)
         for c in self.cases:
             c.analyse_case_declarations(self.subject_clonenode, env)
 
     def analyse_expressions(self, env):
+        sequence_mapping_count = 0
+        for c in self.cases:
+            if c.is_sequence_or_mapping():
+                sequence_mapping_count += 1
+        if sequence_mapping_count >= 2:
+            self.sequence_mapping_temp = AssignableTempNode(
+                self.pos, PyrexTypes.c_uint_type
+            )
+            self.sequence_mapping_temp.is_addressable = lambda: True
+
         self.subject = self.subject.analyse_expressions(env)
         assert isinstance(self.subject, ExprNodes.ProxyNode)
         if not self.subject.arg.is_literal:
             self.subject.arg = self.subject.arg.coerce_to_temp(env)
-        subject = self.subject_clonenode
-        self.cases = [c.analyse_case_expressions(subject, env) for c in self.cases]
+        subject = self.subject_clonenode.analyse_expressions(env)
+        self.cases = [
+            c.analyse_case_expressions(subject, env, self.sequence_mapping_temp)
+            for c in self.cases
+        ]
+        self.cases = [c for c in self.cases if c is not None]
         return self
 
     def generate_execution_code(self, code):
+        if self.sequence_mapping_temp:
+            self.sequence_mapping_temp.allocate(code)
+            code.putln(
+                "%s = 0; /* sequence/mapping test temp */"
+                % self.sequence_mapping_temp.result()
+            )

comment_data.body:
Minor formatting, quotes, etc.
```suggestion
    How these nodes are processed:
    1. During "analyse_declarations", "PatternNode.generate_target_assignments()"
       is called on the main "PatternNode" of the case. This calls its
       sub-patterns ".generate_target_assignments()" recursively.
       This creates a "StatListNode" that is held by the "MatchCaseNode".
    2. In the "analyse_expressions" phases, the "MatchCaseNode" calls
       "PatternNode.analyse_pattern_expressions", which calls its sub-pattern recursively.
    3. At the end of the "analyse_expressions" stage, the "MatchCaseNode"
       class "PatternNode.get_comparison_node()" (which calls
       "PatternNode.get_comparison_node()" for its sub-patterns).
       This returns an ExprNode which can be evaluated to determine if the
       pattern has matched.
       While generating the comparison we try quite hard not to
       analyse it until right at the end, because otherwise it'll lead
       to a lot of repeated work for deeply nested patterns.
    4. In the code generation stage, "PatternNodes" hardly generate any
       code themselves. However, they do set up whatever temps they need
       (mainly for sub-pattern subjects), with "allocate_subject_temps",
       "release_subject_temps", and "dispose_of_subject_temps" (which
       they also call recursively on their sub-patterns).
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -224,29 +287,42 @@ class PatternNode(Node):
 
     Generated in analysis:
     comp_node   ExprNode     node to evaluate for the pattern
+
+    ----------------------------------------
+    How these nodes are processed:
+    1. During "analyse_declarations" PatternNode.generate_target_assignments
+       is called on the main PatternNode of the case. This calls its
+       sub-patterns generate_target_assignments recursively.
+       This creates a StatListNode that is held by the
+       MatchCaseNode.
+    2. In the "analyse_expressions" phases, the MatchCaseNode calls
+       PatternNode.analyse_pattern_expressions, which calls its
+       sub-pattern recursively.
+    3. At the end of the "analyse_expressions" stage the MatchCaseNode
+       class PatternNode.get_comparison_node (which calls
+       PatternNode.get_comparison_node for its sub-patterns). This
+       returns an ExprNode which can be evaluated to determine if the
+       pattern has matched.
+       While generating the comparison we try quite hard not to
+       analyse it until right at the end, because otherwise it'll lead
+       to a lot of repeated work for deeply nested patterns.
+    4. In the code generation stage, PatternNodes hardly generate any
+       code themselves. However, they do set up whatever temps they
+       need (mainly for sub-pattern subjects), with "allocate_subject_temps",
+       "release_subject_temps", and "dispose_of_subject_temps" (which
+       they also call recursively on their sub-patterns)

comment_data.body: Does it _need_ to work on a list? Otherwise, `&PyTuple_GET_ITEM(x, 0)` would probably be faster and use less code, if we already know that we have a tuple.

Since the feature check is supposedly quite quick, we could still check for it in older Python versions and if it's set, return immediately. We allow setting it from Cython now, so it's at least not impossible that it's used.

Also, shouldn't we always be doing the check in the Limited API? We won't know the Python version upfront there.
Rather than a slightly obscure `if (0)` that is not unused code, you could move the label down below the `return` and jump back to `end` afterwards.
`bool`, `complex`, `slice`, sets, others…

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,386 @@
+///////////////////////////// ABCCheck //////////////////////////////
+
+#if PY_VERSION_HEX < 0x030A0000 || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_LIMITED_API || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_PYPY
+static CYTHON_INLINE int __Pyx_MatchCase_IsExactSequence(PyObject *o) {
+    // is one of the small list of builtin types known to be a sequence

comment_data.body:
This is mainly intended as an optimization I think so I didn't intend to be too comprehensive. (It's also in "fallback" code so maybe not important.) The `bytearray`, `bytes`, `str` check above is necessary but these ones aren't

I'll take the view that `bool` is almost certainly worthwhile. I'll add the others you list because they're considerably cheaper than what follow.  I don't think it's worth looking spending too much time being comprehensive here

`Py_TPFLAGS_SEQUENCE` was deliberately not added to the Limited API so the check isn't supposed to be possible there.

However, there is a runtime source of the flag available. I think that might be worthwhile so I'll add that in.

This (and the other changes in ModuleSetupCode.c) are new and should let us do fairly quick checks at runtime on recent versions of Python.

I decided it wasn't ultra-simple to apply them to the indexing code so I haven't done that here. It might be worth a look at some point though.

Or it might be worth moving this to a separate block of utility code and including this only on demand instead of universally.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -760,6 +760,19 @@ static int __Pyx_init_co_variables(void); /* proto */
   #define __Pyx_PyObject_GC_IsFinalized(o) PyObject_GC_IsFinalized(o)
 #endif
 
+#if CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_LIMITED_API

comment_data.body:
I was going to write that we know the macro value and can probably still read the feature flag through the Limited API function, but that might not apply to other implementations of the Limited API. Still a hypothetical issue at this time, though, since there's only CPython that has an implementation.

Did you try asking the `HasFeature` function in the Limited API for the feature? I wouldn't mind taking that route, maybe restricted to CPython's Limited API implementation (if we can detect that).
The "apply changes" interface was really struggling for some reason. It wouldn't let me commit them as a batch earlier

> Still a hypothetical issue at this time, though, since there's only CPython that has an implementation.

I think GraalPy has an ambition to implement it. But hasn't done so yet.

> I was going to write that we know the macro value and can probably still read the feature flag through the Limited API function, but that might not apply to other implementations of the Limited API.

I think in principle CPython could argue that they could change the flag to be a different number in a future Python version. We all know they probably won't. But I'm trying to be nice and respect the flexibility that they're trying to give themselves.
But I have added the check to the Limited API - just with a value that I've extracted at module import (so it should be robust to any future changes)

commit.message:
Initial work to help restore Scanner state

Things like match-case (essentially anything that uses Python's
new PEG parser capacities) are going to have to be implemented
by trying to parse something, failing, then going back and trying
to parse something else. This commit gets the initial work done
to make this easier to do
Fix error string locations in text
Initial work on parsing match statements
Fix a few mistakes in pxd file
Add back in separate scanning position

Basically parses right (but very little else)

Structure it parses into is largely nonsense
Parses the test file without error into a moderately sensible structure

Apply suggestions from code review

Co-authored-by: scoder <stefan_ml@behnel.de>

tree 515fac0e174d8934427ef3a31a695d65ec2a1892
parent 82ec7b45400cd80bd7131defebeefb4a92afa3ce
author da-woods <dw-git@d-woods.co.uk> 1654025257 +0100
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1654025257 +0100

Apply suggestions from code review

Co-authored-by: scoder <stefan_ml@behnel.de>

commit.message:
Really basic patterns work

By being converted into an if statement

Handle simple guards and begin "non-transformed" match

By non-transformed match statement I mean "isn't changed
to a set of if/else statements" - instead has it's own
structure. Variable binding doesn't work yet in these
non-transformed statements
Moved pattern match to its own file
More rational way of handling pattern child_attrs

Handle "group patterns" better

They can generate multiple "as_targets"
Enable more tests, fix identity check
Fix flow control for substituted if statements

Handle assignment to targets

For simple cases at least. This also sets up flow-control
(hopefully correctly)

Added static type checks

Since we can work out in advance that some sequences succeed
Add extra tests and fix some bugs

Partially fix sequence and map type identification

Not possible to fix it completely unfortunately (on earlier
versions of Python)

Improved issequence/ismapping test

Especially with multiple inheritance

Fix one logic error and speed sequence/mapping checks up

Large optimizations of sequence/mapping tests to avoid recalculating
the same slow process multiple times.
Added an extra test for array.array
Optimization for tuples and long generic iterables
Optimization to defer sequence evaluation until needed
Rewrote slicing to index rather than iterate

Improve pattern identification

Some types of pattern are unambiguous so don't need backtracking

Refactoring to avoid re-analysing comparison code

Refactor so that the comparison code only gets run once at the
end, and not repeatedly for each layer.

Move the get_comparison_node call into the CaseNode

Document the process a lot more thoroughly

Convert sequence tests to balanced tree

It should hopefully allow more complicated patterns without overflow

Remove a case that we were mishandling

Difficult to handle or patterns with targets as a simple if
statement
Move some tests to a pure py file
Fix an issue with flow control and add tests

Apply suggestions from code review

Co-authored-by: scoder <stefan_ml@behnel.de>
